data.title: An Analysis Of Tariffs And Inflation: Why I'm Skeptical
data.description:
Introduction

Tariffs are once again back in the news. After Liberation Day sent the markets into a volatile tailspin in April, tariffs were paused until July 9th to give countries time to negotiate. While deals have been made, the deadline has passed, and new tariffs are set to go into effect on August 1st. The issue of trade and tariffs have left many confused, including the Federal Reserve who are cautious to lower rates as tariffs threaten to increase inflation. An examination of recent data provides context as to why trade related inflation hasn’t hit yet and what investors should look out for to hint that inflation may be ahead.

How We Got Here: Trade Deficits

The Trump Administration has taken serious exception to the persistent trade deficits that have existed in the United States for some time but have become increasingly steeper over the last three decades. A trade deficit exists when a country imports more than it exports. This problem was briefly made worse over the winter as importers ratcheted up their imports in advance of a Trump presidency. At last read, the trade deficit stood at close to $100 billion per month. The United States is running a trade surplus when it comes to services, so the entire deficit is consisting of trade involving goods.

Having a Sense of Proportion

Throwing around big numbers like $100 billion per month can be intimidating, but investors should have a sense of proportion about what we are dealing with. The trade deficit, for its size, still comprises barely 4% of US GDP. This is compared to overall personal consumption, which is now 68% of the overall economy.

The changes in personal consumption expenditure (PCE) are what dictate the inflation level, and it is what the Fed uses to determine if prices are within the goals of the Central Bank or if interest rates need to be adjusted. Thus far, on a quarterly basis, personal consumption has continued to gradually fall since the pandemic suggesting that macroeconomic elements are causing demand growth to shrink.

Furthermore, the goods sector has experienced less growth than the remainder of personal consumption, and that trend is not new. The US economy’s goods-based consumption as a share of the overall economy has steadily declined over the last half century with the pandemic being a brief exception. Goods based consumption has gone from one third of the US economy to under one quarter.

Using Tariff Collections to Calculate Inflation

Knowing the size and scope of consumption is beneficial to determining the impact with respect to inflation, but investors also need to know the dollar amount in tariffs that the government collects. Fortunately, the US Treasury reports that information monthly. While the amount appears to be still increasing, as of June the US Treasury is collecting $26 billion in monthly tariffs, $19 billion more than the average monthly levels in 2024.

Based on June numbers, the US government should collect $228 billion in additional tariffs over the next 12 months. While goods imports are a large part of total goods consumption (52%), and overall consumption (16%), it’s the tariff increases themselves that are being passed along to the consumer. The $228 billion in tariffs amounts to 1.1% of consumption over the next 12 months, or 0.1% monthly.

Conclusion

The projected monthly increase of 0.1% jives with what we saw in the CPI number released earlier this week, but most of the increases seen there were in the service sector. The current state of tariff collections is negligible to inflation, and that should continue through next month. As Trump looks to adjust tariff rates for many countries, the August through December collection figures will provide far more clarity as to the impact on consumer prices, but it will take an additional $20 billion per month to move the needle another 0.1%, therefore I’m skeptical that tariffs will ultimately lead to widespread inflation.

data.title: Climbing Limo GDP Forecast For Q2 2025
data.description:
The climbing limo method of forecasting future GDP in the United States projects the nation's economic output in the recently finished second quarter of 2025 will be around $30.5 trillion.

This estimate assumes the momentum the U.S. economy recorded in growing between 2024-Q1 and 2024-Q3 will be sustained through the current quarter. Unfortunately, the U.S. economy's momentum has been slowing, which suggests GDP for 2025-Q2 will come in below that estimate.

Speaking of which, that exact scenario played in the first quarter of 2025, which we can confirm with the third estimate of that quarter's GDP that was reported on 26 June 2025. The climbing limo method had forecast the economy would grow $30.1 trillion in nominal, non-inflation adjusted terms, which we projected three months ago. The actual figure came in at $30.0 trillion, which is not far off from that projection, but still undershoots it. This outcome confirms the nation's nominal economic growth has slowed.

We're citing these figures as if they're fully fixed, but they are still subject to annual revisions. The Bureau of Economic Statistics typically performs an annual revision for its GDP data sometime during the third quarter of each year, most often in July. When it does, we'll regenerate all the climbing limo forecasts based on how the official data changes.

Until then, the following chart shows the climbing limo method's forecast against the recorded nominal GDP over the past 12 quarters for which GDP data has been finalized outside of the BEA's annual revisions.

Regardless of revisions, the GDP data for 2025-Q2 will be something to watch because of the global tariff war that erupted on 2 April 2025 at the start of the quarter. The tariff war may have something of a wildcard effect on the U.S. economy's growth trajectory, hinging on multiple factors that have been playing out within the economy during the last few months.

References

U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis. National Income and Product Accounts. Table 1.1.5. Gross Domestic Product. [Online Database]. Accessed 26 June 2025.

Political Calculations. Forecasting GDP Using the Climbing Limo. [Online Tool]. 10 May 2005.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: IVES: A Watershed Moment For Investors In The AI Revolution
data.description:
Thematic ETFs have taken a new, more nuanced turn in the past few quarters. Last October, I profiled the Fundstrat Granny Shots US Large Cap ETF (GRNY), a portfolio that tracks Tom Lee's (of Fundstrat fame) Granny Shots list of stocks. Lee's fund has done well, returning more than 15% since its November 2024 launch, with more than 4 percentage points of alpha to the Nasdaq 100 ETF (QQQ).

GRNY Lifts to All-Time Highs, Beating QQQ Since Its November 2024 Inception

There's a new kid on the block, and it sports flashy colors and a high-tech allocation. The Dan IVES Wedbush AI Revolution ETF (NYSEARCA:IVES) is an AI-focused and concentrated product. Let's dig into it. I have a buy rating on the fund.

IVES With Alpha Early On

According to the issuer, IVES seeks to capitalize on the rapid growth of artificial intelligence (AI) by investing in companies poised to lead the AI transformation. This fund offers investors exposure to a diversified portfolio of firms at the cutting edge of AI technology.

Dan Ives is a regular guest on CNBC and Bloomberg programming, so readers are likely familiar with his grandiose style. He has a knack for linking compelling narratives to price action and fundamental themes. The IVES allocation draws from Ives' proprietary research behind the "AI Revolution Theme." The ETF is an investable product, capturing a multi-year analysis identifying 30 public companies at the core of the AI spending cycle.

It holds just 30 securities, far fewer than GRNY. So, I expect its returns to be more volatile, while potentially straying further from the Nasdaq 100. IVES is also focused on a handful of innovation industries, including semiconductors, AI hyperscalers, software/cloud platforms, cybersecurity, robotics, and consumer tech.

IVES: Industry and Country Breakdown

IVES Does Hold a Small Amount of Ex-US Equity Exposure

IVES: Industry Spotlights

IVES does not come cheap. Like GRNY, its annual expense ratio is high at 75 basis points, and I expect the dividend yield to be quite low based on the portfolio. At issuance, Seeking Alpha called out the 30 stocks and each of their Seeking Alpha analyst rankings. Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) boasts the best grade, and it reported stellar earnings this week, along with a sanguine outlook. Venture to the bottom of the list, and we'll find a pair of story stocks.

Palantir (PLTR) is the best-performing S&P 500 component so far this year, returning an even 100% YTD through July 16. Its momentum is sky-high, but the AI-defense stock also features very high implied volatility levels, to the tune of 68%, according to data from Option Research & Technology Services (ORATS). PLTR reports earnings on August 4, so that could draw volatility in IVES. Still, PLTR is just 4.2% of the portfolio today.

Tesla (TSLA) is the lowest-rated stock by Seeking Alpha analysts. The embattled EV automaker's stock is down 20% thus far in 2025, and the Mag 7 name has ventured back and forth around the $300 line.

IVES Holdings and Seeking Alpha Analyst Scores

While there are high-volatility stocks and even some possible low-quality names, the largest holdings are stalwarts, which should keep IVES's overall volatility in check. NVIDIA (NVDA) is the biggest position at 5.6%, though that's actually an underweight compared to the chip company's weight in both the S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100.

NVDA is up by more than 19% in the past month, helping IVES to post alpha to both SPY and QQQ since its June 4 inception.

IVES 30-Stock Portfolio

IVES Strong Out of the Gates

Bigger picture, IVES has already attracted more than $340 million in assets under management as of July 16, and I expect it to grow, like GRNY has.

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on the fund. There isn't much of a track record, and we are right at a bearish seasonal inflection point (based on historical price trends), but I like the balance of high-risk-reward plays with anchor stalwarts like NVIDIA and Microsoft (MSFT) in the fund. This is potentially a watershed moment for the AI theme-investors can now access Dan Ives' best ideas on the AI revolution.

Of course, if you are unsure or are uneasy about the high expense ratio, a position in the Nasdaq 100 is a solid, low-cost method to gaining US tech exposure.

data.title: FT: Income Plus Inflation Protection
data.description:
The Franklin Universal Trust (NYSE:FT) is a closed-end fund that aims to provide a very high level of current income to its investors, and as such, it attempts to provide a solution for anyone who is struggling to obtain income in the high-priced, low-yielding environment that has dominated the market for most of the 21st century. Unfortunately, however, this fund’s current yield is not much better than what can be obtained from a junk bond index fund. As of the time of writing, the Franklin Universal Trust yields 6.61%, which as we can see here is only a few basis points higher than junk bonds or preferred stocks:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (AGG)

3.86%

Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index (JNK)

6.61%

ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred and Hybrid Securities Index (PFF)

6.59%

As clearly shown, the Franklin Universal Trust has a yield that is identical to the domestic junk bond index and only two basis points higher than the domestic preferred stock index. Thus, if yield is the only consideration, it is difficult to make a case for this fund as opposed to other things that are available in the market.

However, when we consider that the Franklin Universal Trust does not invest solely in fixed-income securities, we start to see some reasons why an investor might prefer it over a bond index. The fund’s website makes the following comments about the composition of this fund:

The fund focuses on high yield bonds and utility stocks with the goal of providing relatively high yield. We use fundamental research to select a diversified portfolio of high yield corporate bonds and stocks with attractive dividend yields.

The strategy description says that the Franklin Universal Trust invests in both junk bonds and common equities, which makes it a superior option to a pure junk bond fund, all else being equal. After all, as I stated in a previous article, bonds do not provide any sort of protection against inflation. This is because the borrower only has to repay the loan in nominal terms, due to the amount that was lent and the amount that must be returned at maturity not changing with respect to changes in the purchasing power of the currency. This can result in situations like what has occurred over the past decade, in which investment-grade bonds delivered a negative real total return and junk bonds barely delivered a positive real return:

This chart shows the inflation-adjusted returns of the Vanguard Total Bond Market Index Fund (VBMFX) and the SPDR Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF over the past decade, assuming that an investor used all of the distributions paid out by the two index funds to buy additional shares of the fund. As we can see, the investment-grade bond index actually comes in negative, which tells us that investors in that particular index actually lost purchasing power despite reinvesting all the distributions and getting the benefits of compounding. Investors in the domestic junk bond index only managed to grow their purchasing power at a rate of 1% annually on average, which is a very low return for taking on the risk of junk bonds.

As I pointed out earlier today, the most recent inflation report shows that inflation has not been “beaten,” like some market commentators and Federal Reserve cheerleaders like to claim. Housing costs, in particular, continue to accelerate at more than double the Federal Reserve’s 2% target rate, and both core services and core goods are showing signs of accelerating inflation. It is very difficult to recommend owning bonds during any environment in which inflation is not exceptionally low, due to the fact that they provide no protection against the loss of purchasing power that accompanies inflation. If the inflation rate continues to increase as it did in June, it is very possible that many investment-grade bonds will fail to deliver a positive real yield in 2025. It depends on whether the June figures were a blip or a sign of an impending trend (and it is very possible that if the Federal Reserve cuts interest rates as expected, then it will spark a further increase in the inflation rate). While it is highly unlikely that junk bonds will fail to deliver a positive real yield, their lack of any inherent protection against inflation does mean that investors might not get a real yield commensurate with the risk of investing in such assets.

Common equities, on the other hand, do not have this problem. Equities represent ownership of the issuing company, not merely debt owed by the company. Most companies are able to increase their prices whenever inflation sets in, which results in their profits rising alongside inflation. If the company’s valuation multiples remain stable, then the price of their equities should rise and allow investors to maintain their purchasing power. This is especially true for utilities, as regulators allow them to raise rates at least at a pace equivalent to inflation. When we combine that with the generally stable financial profile of most utilities, we get an almost ideal holding for investors who want an inflation-resistant holding that pays a higher yield than most equities. The Franklin Universal Trust combines junk bonds with utilities in an attempt to build an “all-weather” portfolio that both provides a high yield and has some protection against both inflation and recessions. However, the bulk of the fund’s portfolio is invested in junk bonds right now:

This is not ideal to my mind. I would be much happier with either a 50/50 split between fixed-income and equity or having equity account for more than 50% of the profile for added protection against the almost certain devaluation of the U.S. dollar and the inflation that will result from such an event. With that said, though, the Franklin Universal Trust does have a better asset allocation than most bond funds, and it might be okay for someone who believes that interest rates will start declining in the near future.

Peer Distribution Comparison

The current yield of the Franklin Universal Trust is not particularly attractive when compared to other closed-end funds that invest primarily in junk bonds. This is probably not very surprising given that this fund’s yield is comparable to that of the domestic junk bond index, and most closed-end funds are able to deliver better yields than comparable indices. As we can see here, the difference between the yield of the Franklin Universal Trust and most junk bond funds is quite large:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

Franklin Universal Trust

Hybrid-U.S. Allocation

6.61%

AllianceBernstein Global High Income Fund (AWF)

Fixed Income – Taxable-High Yield

7.13%

Allspring Income Opportunities Fund (EAD)

Fixed Income – Taxable-High Yield

9.06%

BNY Mellon High Yield Strategies Fund (DHF)

Fixed Income – Taxable-High Yield

8.11%

BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund (HYT)

Fixed Income – Taxable-High Yield

9.58%

Invesco High Income Trust II (VLT)

Fixed Income – Taxable-High Yield

10.49%

Western Asset High Income Fund II (HIX)

Fixed Income – Taxable-High Yield

13.67%

As we can immediately see, the Franklin Universal Trust has a significantly lower yield than most junk bond funds. However, this is not an apples-to-apples comparison due to the simple fact that the Franklin Universal Trust invests its assets in both junk bonds and common equities of utility companies. As of the time of writing, the Utilities Select Sector Index (XLU) only yields 2.78%, which is far lower than the yield of junk bonds. Thus, the fact that roughly a third of the fund’s assets are invested in the common stock of utility companies reduces the yield that the Franklin Universal Trust receives from its portfolio compared to what would be if the case if the Franklin Universal Trust were invested entirely in junk bonds.

When we compare the yield of the Franklin Universal Trust to that of some other closed-end funds that invest in both debt and equity securities, its yield is more competitive. We can see that here:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

Franklin Universal Trust

Hybrid-U.S. Allocation

6.61%

Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund (ERH)

Hybrid-U.S. Allocation

8.39%

Bexil Investment Trust (OTCPK:BXSY)

Hybrid-U.S. Allocation

7.41%

Calamos Strategic Total Return Fund (CSQ)

Hybrid-U.S. Allocation

6.76%

Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund (EVT)

Hybrid-U.S. Allocation

8.24%

John Hancock Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund (HTD)

Hybrid-U.S. Allocation

7.91%

We still see that the current yield of the Franklin Universal Trust is the lowest out of this peer group. However, in this case, the difference between its yield and the yield of the second-lowest-yielding fund in the category is only fifteen basis points. That is not a particularly large difference, and, in fact, it is small enough that a difference between the relative movement of the two funds could erase it. Thus, that may not be a big enough difference for us to really worry about.

In a few previous articles, we have seen that some low-yielding funds manage to deliver fairly significant capital gains and thus have a very respectable total return despite their low yields. The Calamos Strategic Total Return Fund is a good example of a fund that performs in this way. Unfortunately for potential investors, though, the Franklin Universal Trust has not delivered a particularly impressive performance over the past decade. As we can see here, the fund only delivered a 131.18% total return over the trailing ten-year period:

This was notably worse than most of the other funds in its peer group. In fact, the only fund shown that did worse was the Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund. This is certainly disappointing, and it works against our goal of trying to preserve our purchasing power in the face of inflation since the higher a fund’s total return, the better it manages to accomplish that task. With that said, though, the Franklin Universal Trust did still manage to deliver a positive total real return over the past ten years:

After adjusting for inflation and assuming reinvestment of all distributions, the Franklin Universal Trust still managed to grow its investors’ purchasing power by 65.17% over the trailing ten-year period, or at an average rate of 5.15% per year. That was much better than either domestic investment-grade or domestic junk bonds managed to deliver over the same period, so we can still see the general superiority of this fund over bond indices despite the fact that its yield is not much higher than that of domestic junk bonds. For retirees in particular, the fact that this fund manages to beat inflation is very attractive.

The fact that the Franklin Universal Trust was one of the worst-performing funds in its category over the past decade will likely still be a turn-off for many investors, however. But it is always important to keep in mind that past performance is no guarantee of future results. It is possible that the fund will perform better going forward than it has in the past, so we should take a closer look at it and see how it is positioned and likely to perform going forward.

Recent Performance Update

The last time that we discussed the Franklin Universal Trust was on May 14, 2024. As such, approximately fourteen months have passed since we last looked at the fund, and, as is often the case in the financial markets, a great many things occurred over the trailing fourteen-month period that undoubtedly had an impact on the performance of the Franklin Universal Trust.

One of the more important events that occurred over the past fourteen months is that the Federal Reserve reduced its benchmark federal funds target rate by 100 basis points. This came about through a series of interest rate cuts at the September 2024, November 2024, and December 2024 meetings. The market was expecting this for a while, though, so it did not have the effect of raising bond prices (and lowering yields) that many people expected. Back in August of 2024, bonds were already priced for the interest rate cuts, so they did not rise following the interest rate cut. In fact, bond prices fell, and yields started to rise due mostly to the market widely expecting that the Federal Reserve’s interest rate cuts would cause an acceleration in inflation, which did actually happen. As we can see here, the headline Consumer Price Index started increasing at an accelerated annual rate over the entire period that the central bank was reducing interest rates:

Due to the fact that bonds offer no inherent protection against inflation, bond prices tend to fall whenever expectations about inflation rise. This is particularly true at the long end of the yield curve, due to the simple fact that the Federal Reserve does not directly determine the interest rate on long-dated bonds. This chart shows the yield of the ten-year, twenty-year, and thirty-year U.S. Treasury securities on September 18, 2024 (the date of the Federal Reserve’s interest rate cut) and at the end of 2024:

Bond Term

Yield on September 18, 2024

Yield on December 31, 2024

Ten-Year

3.70%

4.58%

Twenty-Year

4.08%

4.86%

Thirty-Year

4.03%

4.78%

Thus, the Federal Reserve’s attempts to reduce interest rates actually had the opposite effect, and yields rose. Investors began to expect that inflation would be higher going forward, so they started to demand higher yields in order to offset the decline in purchasing power that would result from that inflation. This continues to be the case today, and yields on the twenty-year and thirty-year U.S. Treasury bonds are even higher than they were at the start of the year.

Curiously, the fall in long-dated investment-grade bond prices did not drag down junk bonds as much as might be expected. In fact, domestic junk bonds are actually up over the past twelve months:

This is mostly pointing to a narrowing of the risk premium between the two types of bonds. Basically, one of two things is happening:

  1. Investors no longer believe that U.S. Treasuries are “risk-free.”
  2. Investors think that the economy has strengthened considerably over the past twelve months, which has reduced the risk that junk bond issuers will default on their debt obligations.

It is difficult to know which of the two options is correct, but given the fact that all three rating agencies have now stripped the United States of its AAA credit rating, I am leaning towards the first option being the most likely explanation. Perhaps a caveat should be added to that, though, as I doubt that the United States government will default on its obligations on a nominal basis, but it is a very real risk that the currency that it will repay long-dated bonds with will be worth a lot less than it is worth today.

The Franklin Universal Trust invests in junk bonds, not investment-grade bonds, so the general decline in yields should not have much impact on its bond holdings. Furthermore, if the market is expecting higher levels of inflation going forward, which is what the rise in long-term yields suggests, then utility stocks could be a beneficiary of that due to the ease with which utility companies can raise their prices in response to inflation. While utilities are sometimes considered to be a proxy for bonds, they are very different in this respect and that can lead to performance differences. We can certainly see this over the past year, as the Utilities Select Sector Index is up 17.07%, substantially beating either type of bond:

As we already saw, the Franklin Universal Trust has just over a third of its assets invested in utilities. Thus, this portion of its portfolio would have benefited from the gains here. While the exact percentage that the fund holds in both asset classes (junk bonds and utilities) likely varied somewhat over the past year, the fact that both asset classes are up should mean that the fund’s shares have also risen since our last discussion on this fund.

This is indeed the case, as shares of the Franklin Universal Trust have appreciated by 12.80% since our last discussion on the fund:

As we can see, the Franklin Universal Trust substantially outperformed both domestic investment-grade bonds and domestic junk bonds over the period. However, it did underperform the utility sector index, which tracks common equities issued by the largest utility companies in the United States. This is pretty much what we expected, as the utilities held in the fund’s portfolio should have boosted its performance above that of junk bonds, while the junk bonds dragged down the performance compared to an all-utility equity portfolio.

When we look at total return, as opposed to price performance, the Franklin Universal Trust looks much better:

As we can see, investors in this fund have received a total return of 22.36% since our last discussion on it. This is a much better total return than any of the indices delivered over the same period, which is also not particularly surprising considering that the fund delivered equity-like performance and has a similar yield to the junk bond index. Overall, investors in this fund are likely to be fairly satisfied with its performance over the past fourteen months.

About The Fund

The website for the Franklin Universal Trust states:

The fund seeks to provide high current income consistent with preservation of capital. Its secondary objective is growth of income through dividend increases and capital appreciation.

This actually makes a lot of sense given the fund’s strategy of investing in both junk bonds and utility stocks. Junk bonds, as I have mentioned in various past articles, are an income vehicle and primarily deliver their total return through the coupons that they pay to their owners. Utilities, on the other hand, are not. While they do tend to have higher yields than the average stock in the S&P 500 Index (SP500), their current yields are well below that of most money market funds. If we look back over the past ten years, we can see that most of the investment returns that utilities delivered were in the form of capital appreciation and not dividends:

As we can see, the utility sector delivered a total return of 167.87% over the past decade. Of that total, 93.69% (more than half) came from share price appreciation and not the dividends that the sector paid out. Thus, utilities are not an income vehicle in the same way that bonds are.

The secondary objective of the Franklin Universal Trust, though, is to provide growth of income through dividend increases and capital appreciation. That is very compatible with a utility portfolio due to the simple fact that many utilities have the goal of increasing their dividend at least annually. Thus, the fund will naturally grow its income as the utility stocks that it holds increase their dividends. Of course, this requires that the utility stocks in the fund’s portfolio increase their dividends on a regular basis, but most of them do. We can see this by looking at the largest positions in the fund. The website states that the Franklin Universal Trust was holding the following as its largest positions on June 30, 2025:

This chart shows the rate at which each of these utilities has grown its dividend over the past five years (the five-year compound annual growth rate):

Company

5-Yr. Dividend Growth Rate (CAGR)

NextEra Energy (NEE)

10.30%

Sempra (SRE)

4.68%

Entergy (ETR)

5.03%

Evergy (EVRG)

5.86%

Alliant Energy (LNT)

6.08%

Duke Energy (DUK)

2.03%

The Southern Co. (SO)

3.01%

CenterPoint Energy (CNP)

-3.49%

CMS Energy (CMS)

6.01%

With the exception of CenterPoint Energy, all of these companies have managed to deliver dividend growth over the past five years, and most of them have managed to deliver annual increases. The rates at which they have grown their dividends certainly varied, though, with companies such as NextEra Energy and Alliant Energy doing much better than Duke Energy or The Southern Company. Nevertheless, any dividend growth at all is better than the static coupons that bonds provide. After all, anyone who wants to live off of the income that their portfolio provides needs regular growth if they simply wish to maintain their living standards as inflation drives prices ever higher. Unfortunately for investors, though, the Franklin Universal Trust has not done an especially good job at passing through dividend increases from its portfolio companies to its investors via its own distributions. The fund has paid a steady $0.0425 per share monthly distribution since 2021. Thus, investors who have wanted to grow their own incomes through the use of this fund have had to reinvest at least some of the distributions that it has paid out. This is the case with most closed-end funds, so it is not really worth complaining about, but it has not been as good at growing its distribution as some other funds that have utility exposure, such as the Reaves Utility Income Fund (UTG).

The fund’s failure to pass along the dividend increases to its shareholders does not appear to be due to harvesting capital gains. Indeed, this fund appears to rarely realize capital gains. The fund’s most recent semi-annual report states that the Franklin Universal Trust only had a 9.67% turnover for the six-month period that ended on February 28, 2025. The fund’s turnover during previous full-year periods is similarly low, as we can see here:

FY 2024

FY 2023

FY 2022

FY 2021

FY 2020

Portfolio Turnover

21.79%

17.73%

22.29%

36.58%

35.26%

(all figures are for the full-year period that ended on August 31 of the respective year)

These are not particularly high turnover ratios, as anyone who holds other funds will attest to. As such, it does not appear that this fund is selling assets and then distributing the capital gains to investors. Rather, it appears to be holding assets for extended periods of time and collecting the dividends.

Despite this, there have been a few changes to the fund’s largest positions list since the last time that we discussed this fund. In particular, the Franklin Universal Trust no longer counts American Electric Power (AEP) or Constellation Energy (CEG) among its largest positions. In their place, we have Entergy Corporation, which serves much of East Texas and Louisiana, and a large position in a money market fund. This suggests that the fund likely sold out of some of its positions and is now waiting for an attractive buying opportunity. From an income perspective, of course, the money market fund actually has a higher yield than most utility stocks, so it does not appear to be hurting its income by waiting. It might be sacrificing capital gains, though, should the market continue to rally.

Distribution Analysis

The primary objective of the Franklin Universal Trust is to provide a high level of current income to its investors. In accordance with this objective, the fund pays a monthly distribution of $0.0425 per share ($0.51 per share annually). This gives the fund a 6.61% yield at the current share price, which is not especially impressive compared to its peers in the market.

As already mentioned, this fund has been fairly consistent with its distributions over the past few years, but we should still have a look at its finances to see how easily it can actually afford the payments. For this purpose, we will consult the fund’s semi-annual report for the six-month period that ended on February 28, 2025. As might be expected, this is a much newer report than what we had available to us the last time that we discussed this fund.

For the six-month period that ended on February 28, 2025, the Franklin Universal Trust reported a net investment income of $4,516,008. That was not sufficient to cover the $6,408,633 that the fund paid out in distributions over the same period.

The fund was able to make up for the difference through capital gains, however. For the six-month period that ended on February 28, 2025, the Franklin Universal Trust reported net realized gains of $4,436,077, which were partially offset by $919,308 of net unrealized losses. This all gives the fund a net total of $8,032,777 in investment profits over the period:

That was sufficient to cover all of the distributions that the fund paid out over the period. It was also the case that this fund fully covered its distributions for the full-year period that ended on August 31, 2024. Thus, it is not having any trouble carrying its distribution at the current level, and there is no apparent reason to worry about a near-term distribution cut.

Valuation

Shares of the Franklin Universal Trust are currently trading at a 6.86% discount on net asset value. This is a bit worse than the 7.86% discount that the fund’s shares have had on average over the past month, but the current price still represents a discount on the actual value of the financial assets that are underlying each share.

The fund’s current price is reasonable compared to its historical pricing. Over the past three years, the shares of the Franklin Universal Trust have traded at prices ranging from a 0.99% discount to a 14.32% discount on net asset value. The average price over the period was a 9.91% discount on net asset value. Admittedly, the average price over the three-year period was better than what the fund has today. However, we can see that the fund is nowhere near its three-year high.

Here is how the current price of this fund compares with its peers:

Fund Name

Premium/(Discount)

Franklin Universal Trust

(6.86%)

Allspring Utilities and High Income Fund

(8.85%)

Bexil Investment Trust

(36.64%)

Calamos Strategic Total Return Fund

(3.19%)

Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund

(7.49%)

John Hancock Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund

(4.03%)

The median valuation of these six funds is a 7.18% discount, so we can see that this fund is currently priced at a level that is a bit higher than the peer median. However, when we consider that it is pretty far from its three-year high, it is probably not too bad. More cautious investors might want to wait for the discount to increase to around 10% though, as that would be roughly in line with this fund’s long-term average.

Expenses

The Franklin Universal Trust has an expense ratio of 3.32%:

The fund’s semi-annual report states that this ratio dropped to 3.00% for the six-month period that ended on February 28, 2025.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Franklin Universal Trust has a strategy that income-seeking investors might find attractive. The fact that this fund includes hefty exposure to utilities gives it some inflation protection that most junk bond funds lack. The presence of utilities also allows the fund to deliver some of its returns in the form of capital gains, which can be nice at tax time. However, it is still worth noting that the fund has underperformed a lot of its peers, and it only has about a third of its portfolio invested in equities. As such, it may not be the best choice in the sector, but it is an interesting fund that should not be ignored.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 2 REITs To Buy Before September 17th, 2025
data.description:
Rate cuts are back on the table.

After a 7-month pause in cuts, the debt market is now predicting that the Fed will likely cut interest rates at their meeting on September 17th.

That's just two months away.

If not then, the probability of a rate cut is even higher by October 29th. The market is currently assigning a near 90% chance of at least one rate cut by then:

I am bringing this up because some investments have a lot to gain from these rate cuts.

REITs (VNQ), in particular, have been heavily discounted for most of the last 3 years, and today, they continue to trade at decade-low valuations almost entirely due to the surge in interest rates.

If rate hikes caused their valuations to crash, then rate cuts are likely to lead to a recovery. And to prove this point, consider that REITs have already surged by 30% on average since the Fed began cutting rates in late 2023:

You will note from the above chart that they gave a portion of their gains during this pause, but the recovery is likely to resume now as interest rates continue to decline.

The debt market is today pricing 4 rate cuts within a year from now:

Morgan Stanley is even more aggressive, expecting 7 rate cuts by the end of 2026. The reason why is that real-time inflation has now been below 2% for two years. Tariffs could cause a burst of inflation, but it is expected to be temporary and modest. Finally, the economy is expected to weaken as a result of these tariffs and the uncertainty they create.

That would be incredibly bullish for certain REITs that have recession-resistant business models, but are heavily discounted due to the high interest rates.

In today's article, I am going to highlight two such opportunities that offer significant upside potential.

The first one is a safer bet. The second one is a more aggressive option for investors with a higher risk tolerance.

NNN REIT, Inc. (NNN)

NNN is our Top Pick among net lease REITs right now at High Yield Landlord.

A lot of investors prefer to stick with Realty Income (O) in this sector, but we think that NNN (NNN) is superior, and we are going to explain to you why.

Both focus on what we call "triple net lease" properties. These are typically standalone single-tenant retail properties that are occupied by service-oriented tenants such as quick service restaurants, convenience stores, and car washes:

We call them net lease properties because the lease is typically structured in a way that provides highly consistent and predictable cash flow to the landlord.

  • Very long lease terms at 15-20 years.
  • Automatic annual rent escalations of about 2%
  • No landlord responsibilities, meaning that tenants are responsible for all property expenses, including property taxes and maintenance.

It results in bond-like cash flow, and as a result, net lease REITs have historically been highly sensitive to interest rates.

When rates surge, net lease REITs (NETL) underperform, and vice versa. You can see here how this has impacted its valuation in recent years:

Note that this is despite having managed to consistently grow its cash flow and dividend.

As a result, its valuation is now historically low, trading at just 12.5x funds from operations, or FFO, and offering a 5.4% dividend yield. In comparison, it traded at 22x FFO before the start of the REIT bear market.

And why is NNN our Top Pick among net lease REITs?

In short, it offers the best price-to-quality ratio.

It is only slightly more expensive than lower-quality peers like EPR Properties (EPR) and Getty Realty (GTY), which own a lot of troubled assets like movie theaters and gas stations.

And it is much cheaper than most other peers, including Agree Realty (ADC) and Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT), and even Realty Income (O), despite enjoying characteristics that are typically worth a premium.

We believe that NNN is a true blue-chip REIT that belongs at the top of the valuation range in the net lease peer group, and here are three simple reasons why:

1) Exceptional track record: Unlike many of its close peers that have shorter track records (EPRT) and/or have suffered some missteps along the way (ADC), NNN has a perfect 30+ year track record of steady dividend growth and consistent market outperformance. Not even the great financial crisis or the pandemic could stop it:

2) Superior balance sheet: NNN has a BBB+ investment grade rating with a low 35% LTV and, importantly, its debt maturities are the longest in the net lease peer group at 12 years. Therefore, it is less impacted by the surge in interest rates than its peers, and as a result, its low valuation makes even less sense. In comparison, Realty Income only has 6 years left on average on its debt maturities.

3) Steady predictable growth: Finally, NNN owns a high-quality portfolio of mostly service-oriented net lease properties, and it holds little exposure to riskier segments like pharmacies, dollar stores, movie theaters, or gas stations. Its properties enjoy steady rent growth, and it retains about 30% of its cash flow to reinvest in new properties, resulting in consistent cash flow and dividend growth.

We think that these blue-chip characteristics should be rewarded with a premium valuation, and yet, NNN is today offered at a discount to its peers.

As interest rates are cut, I expect all net lease REITs to enjoy upside, but NNN has even more upside potential than the rest.

We expect 30% upside, and while we wait, we earn a 5.4% dividend yield.

Even ignoring the upside from repricing, NNN should be in a position to deliver double-digit total returns from its yield and growth alone.

That's a great risk-to-reward coming from a conservative blue-chip REIT.

Star Holdings (STHO)

Star Holdings is a riskier choice, but it has far more upside potential.

If rates are cut significantly as we expect, Star could turn into a multi-bagger over the coming years.

The reason is that it is essentially a leveraged bet on lower interest rates. The company owns a large stake in the ground lease REIT, Safehold (SAFE), as well as some other development assets.

Ground leases are a type of land lease in which an investor leases the land below an existing building or with the intention of developing a new property.

Such arrangements can be win-win: the tenant gets to control a property for a very long duration without having to pay for the land, while the landlord gets highly secure rental income for a long time to come. If the tenant defaults, the property typically reverts back to the land owner, making lease defaults extremely unlikely.

Ground leases are extremely sensitive to interest rates because their terms are very long, at 99 years initially in most cases. As such, they are similar to ultra-long-term bonds.

SAFE is even more sensitive because it is leveraged. This caused its valuation to crash as interest rates surged:

But as rates are cut, it could recover with a vengeance.

And here is the interesting thing:

Today, the value of Star's stake in SAFE is $204 million, but Star's entire market cap is less than half of that:

Value of stake in SAFE STHO's entire market cap
$207 million $105 million

What this means is that by buying STAR, you get exposure to SAFE at a much lower valuation, and you then get the additional legacy assets on top of that for free.

Put differently, each dollar invested in STAR gives you more than two dollars worth of SAFE, plus some other assets. It is the cheapest it has ever been relative to SAFE since the spinoff.

Star is now in the process of liquidating its legacy development assets, which we expect to be more than enough to cover its debt. It may take a few years, but they are making progress, and rate cuts would surely help them in this process.

Beyond that, we expect it to finish the liquidation by distributing its shares of SAFE to shareholders, removing the current discount. This alone could double your money.

By then, the value of those shares could also have risen significantly if rates had been cut. Even if SAFE's share price doubled from here, it would still trade at a steep discount to its peak levels.

So there is plenty of upside potential here if interest rates return to lower levels, and this is why we maintain a small position as part of a well-diversified portfolio at High Yield Landlord.

data.title: Introducing The Global X Treasury Bond Enhanced Income ETF
data.description:
On July 16, 2025, we listed the Global X Treasury Bond Enhanced Income ETF (TLTX) on the Cboe BZX exchange. TLTX seeks to offer investors exposure to the long end of the Treasury yield curve while writing weekly call options on long-duration Treasury ETFs. The fund aims at writing call options at a value equal to a portion of its notional holdings in an attempt to provide access to a portion of the price appreciation potential of long-duration treasuries. Meanwhile, it is designed to harvest option premiums as a potential source of income.

Investors have long sought to incorporate Treasury exposure into their portfolios. It offers a perceived means of safety being backed by the full faith and credit of the U.S. government, a tax-efficient source of income with exemptions common at state and local levels, and yield potential, which, as of late, has risen to levels not seen for the better part of the last decade. Historically, when investors have looked to enhance the yield associated with these positions, they’ve sought to increase bond duration by moving further out on the yield curve. While longer-duration Treasuries can offer this enhanced yield potential, they also have the potential to carry higher volatility, given their heightened sensitivity to moving interest rates.

The Global X Treasury Bond Enhanced Income ETF seeks to allow investors to maintain exposure to this higher-yielding segment of the curve while potentially mitigating associated volatility through monthly distributions.

TLTX Aims to Address Challenges Facing Income Investors Through a Covered Call Strategy

Within Treasury markets - where most instruments share similarly high credit ratings - the positive relationship between yield and duration is evident (i.e., longer durations generally produce higher yields). The relationship holds true across other fixed income asset classes as well, but when looking at the broader bond market, investors generally have two options when they want to boost yield potential: increase duration or invest further down on the credit-quality spectrum.

Each approach presents distinct trade-offs. Extending duration may expose investors to greater volatility through heightened interest rate sensitivity. Conversely, accepting lower credit quality may result in less volatility relative to long-duration instruments, but it introduces higher default risk as credit quality deteriorates. The Global X Treasury Bond Enhanced Income ETF seeks to address this challenge by allowing investors to take share in the long end of the Treasury yield curve, while seeking to mitigate interest rate sensitivity through the writing of weekly call options. The fund employs a systematic active strategy, but at large it will see the fund hold long positions in a basket of Treasury securities with an approximate 20-year duration target while writing weekly call options, in succession, in an effort to manage volatility associated with the position by cushioning downside price movements with option premiums. The fund is expected to perform monthly distributions, valued at approximately 75% of the weekly premiums it collects. Returns generated by the fund may also be enhanced by the underlying Treasury securities that it holds.

Long-Duration Treasury Volatility Creates Opportunity to Source Better Premiums

Interest rates and economic uncertainty have the potential to influence the value of Treasury securities, and their impact can be even more emphatic at the long end of the curve. This stems from the understanding that bond values are derived from the present value of their future cash flows. Meaning that if the market growth is expected to be healthy for the foreseeable future, the value of a bond’s predetermined coupon payments may prove less impactful than if the market was assuming a weak or negative growth trajectory. That type of environment would likely result in more spending power to the coupon payments provided by the bond. And because long-tenored bonds offer more coupon payments, and more of those payments take place much further out into the future, the influence of interest rates and economic uncertainty tend to have a stronger effect on these longer-dated securities. The level of sensitivity that these long-duration instruments have to changes in the market outlook, among other factors, can be examined by looking at the deviations between monthly total returns provided by 20+ year treasuries over the last 20 years, relative to shorter-duration instruments.

This volatility dynamic creates an interesting opportunity, as volatility typically correlates positively with option premiums. To examine this relationship further, the chart below uses a Black-Scholes1 option pricing model using a weekly call writing strategy on the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT). The analysis clearly demonstrates that higher asset volatility generates correspondingly higher option premiums.

The Global X Treasury Bond Enhanced Income ETF leans into this relationship and the elevated volatility characteristic of long-duration Treasury instruments. It also seeks to keep a portion of its portfolio unencumbered by the influence of written call options to retain price appreciation potential should the value of its underlying treasuries rise. When operating a covered call strategy, the strike price at which the call option can be exercised sets a cap on the upside potential that can be realized by holding the underlying security. By writing the call on only a portion of its portfolio, however, TLTX keeps a measure of the portfolio’s upside uncapped, making the fund a potential total return solution.

A critical consideration by TLTX is that notional option coverage is not determined by the fund's total asset value, but rather by a strategic coverage ratio. The fund typically maintains notional coverage equivalent to approximately half its duration exposure. Since bond prices typically share a negative relationship with interest rates, we expect writing call options at a notional value that represent half the fund’s duration will capture roughly half the upside potential of its Treasury holdings when interest rates are on the decline while collecting option premiums. On the other hand, when interest rates are on the rise, the fund remains exposed to the full downside of its Treasury positions, with option premiums providing a partial offset.

TLTX’s Active Management Team has Discretion to Seek Opportunistic Pricing and Coverage

Many of the functions of TLTX’s investment strategy could be deemed deliberate and systematic. For instance, the fund seeks to utilize weekly call options with consistency as opposed to longer-dated options in an effort to attract higher annualized premiums. This rationale stems from option contracts typically losing value more rapidly the closer that they get to expiration. As a function of this more rapid time value decay, the seller is typically afforded more generous premium compensation when transacting the trade. Thus, by selling short-term weekly options in succession rather than longer-term options, the fund can potentially capture this accelerated decrease in value more efficiently.

Outside these systematic functions, however, the fund’s active portfolio managers are granted the flexibility to perform their trades utilizing varying levels of moneyness and notional coverage, while seeking opportunistic timing in an effort to manage risk and seek out advantageous pricing. The call options that are being written by TLTX are expected to have strike prices that are only slightly out of the money, and the degree of notional that is covered is expected to remain relatively consistent at around half the portfolio’s duration. However, having the flexibility to actively govern these factors may open up the opportunity to enhance returns.

Conclusion: TLTX May Enhance Income Potential Associated with Long-Duration Treasuries

Treasury investment returns fundamentally depend on the interest rates investors can capture from these instruments. The Global X Treasury Bond Enhanced Income ETF seeks to enhance this income potential by systematically writing call options. The premiums received from writing the options can help buffer against capital losses in environments when interest rates rise, or act as a supplement to the returns of a basket of long-duration Treasury securities when the rate environment is static. The fund has the potential to be utilized as a single source of distributions, or as a pairing with a broader basket of treasuries to help manage volatility and maintain upside potential.

Footnotes

  1. The Black Scholes model is a flagship model to calculate the fair price for an option contract using current asset price, expected dividends, strike price of an option, expected interest rates, time to expiration and expected volatility. The model assumes that dividends are not paid, the option can only be exercised at expiration, risk-free rate and volatility of the underlying investment are known and constant, no transaction costs are involved in buying the option, markets are efficient, and the returns are log-normally distributed.

Glossary

Duration: A measure of interest rate sensitivity for a fixed income instrument, denoting an estimate of how much a bond’s price will change in response to a 1% change in interest rates. The greater the duration, the more sensitive the bond.

Notional: The full value of an underlying instrument that could theoretically be tied to a derivatives contract.

STRIPS: Separate Trading of Registered Interest and Principal Securities (STRIPS) are zero-coupon treasury securities that are created by financial institutions to separate coupons from the principal of a U.S. Treasury bond.

Moneyness: A measure of intrinsic value of an option, that is, it will tell the option holder whether exercising the option will be profitable.

Out-of-the-Money: Options that, if exercised, would require the payment of more money than the value received and therefore would not be currently exercised.

At-the-Money: An option in which the underlying asset’s price equals the strike price.

Tenor: An option contract’s tenor is the full length of time over which it will be active and tradable prior to expiration.

Disclosures

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Fixed income securities are subject to loss of principal during periods of rising interest rates. An investment in the fund is not insured or guaranteed by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation or any other government agency. TLTX is non-diversified.

TLTX engages in options trading. An option is a contract sold by one party to another that gives the buyer the right, but not the obligation, to buy (call) or sell (put) a security at an agreed upon price within a certain period or on a specific date. A covered call option involves holding a long position in a particular asset and writing a call option on that same asset with the goal of realizing additional income from the option premium. By selling covered call options, the fund limits its opportunity to profit from an increase in the price of the underlying asset above the exercise price, but continues to bear the risk of a decline in the asset. A liquid market may not exist for options held by the fund. While the fund receives premiums for writing the call options, the price it realizes from the exercise of an option could be substantially below the asset’s current market price.

Shares of ETFs are bought and sold at market price (not NAV) and are not individually redeemed from the Fund. Brokerage commissions will reduce returns.

Index returns are for illustrative purposes only and do not represent actual Fund performance. Index returns do not reflect any management fees, transaction costs or expenses. Indexes are unmanaged and one cannot invest directly in an index. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Carefully consider the Fund’s investment objectives, risks, and charges and expenses before investing. This and other information can be found in the Fund’s summary or full prospectuses, which is available at globalxetfs.com. Please read the prospectus carefully before investing.

Global X Management Company LLC serves as an advisor to Global X Funds. The Funds are distributed by SEI Investments Distribution Co., which is not affiliated with Global X Management Company LLC or Mirae Asset Global Investments.

Original Post

data.title: Double-Checking The Credit Rating (Part 3): Hudson Pacific Properties
data.description:
Co-authored by Strategic Yield.

We continue our series with Hudson Pacific Properties, Inc. (NYSE:HPP), a REIT that serves dynamic technology and media tenants in global epicenters, providing high-quality and well-equipped office and studio spaces.

Overview

The company was founded in 2006, and it is headquartered in Los Angeles, California. Its portfolio comprises 6 properties and 14.7 million square feet. The total assets at the end of Q1 2025 are close to $8 billion, and the current market capitalization is approximately $1.07 billion.

HPP has an asset coverage ratio of 173%. It generates approximately 9% gross rental income. Operating expenses (excluding depreciation) amount to approximately 65.5% of total revenues. When calculating the asset yield (by dividing total revenues by the value of assets), a value of 10.24% is obtained. However, for a more accurate assessment, a market adjustment is applied using the price-to-book value (P/B) ratio, which is currently 0.17. After this market adjustment, the calculated asset yield reaches 15%, and the market-adjusted asset coverage ratio increases to approximately 117%, which is extremely low.

The current market price of HPP's common is $2.78:

Here are the basic valuation metrics:

The forward P/FFO is 13.30, so the expected FFO yield comes to 7.52%.

Fitch rates HPP with a B+ credit rating:

Moody's rating is from Ba3 to Ba1, and S&P with a credit rating of B:

Preferred stock

The preferred stock of HPP (NYSE:HPP.PR.C) has a current yield of 8.12% and a current market price of $14.68. Its call date is after 1.34 years, on 11/16/2026. Moody's rates it with Caa1, S&P with CCC, and Fitch with a B-credit rating. It pays cumulative distributions of 4.75% per annum in the form of $0.296875 quarterly, or $1.1875 annual amount. HPP issued 16 million shares from the stock on 11/04/2021.

OTC bonds

HPP has four unsecured bonds, as you can see below in the chart:

Their yield range is from 6.35% to 8.7%. All have a credit rating of B2 from Moody's. The maturity range is between 2 and 5 years.

Credit rating

Let's now look at why HPP has such a bad rating. As our readers know, we use Moody's approach to track companies' credit ratings. We remind you of what it looks like in the picture below:

1. Total assets: The company has assets worth approximately $8 billion. According to Moody’s methodology, this is classified as Baa1.

2. Unencumbered assets: The unencumbered assets are around 76.5% of the gross assets. It receives a Baa1 equivalent credit rating.

3. Secured debt: The level of secured debt is classified as Baa3.

4. Total debt+preferred stock to total assets: A ratio of 47% results in a Ba3 rating, which signals a moderate degree of indebtedness.

5. Net debt to EBITDA: With EBITDA of $246 million and a net debt/EBITDA ratio of 18.7, this metric receives a rating of Ca3.

6. Fixed charge coverage: EBITDA covers interest expenses and the preferred dividend once, and this ratio leads to a Ba3 rating.

The overall score of these indicators is 12.95, which corresponds to a Ba3 rating. After correction, the score is 14.22, which is equivalent to B1.

Market Positioning and Asset Quality

HPP operates primarily in a highly regulated sector, which creates some uncertainty due to various restrictions; for this reason, it receives a rating of 18, or the equivalent of Caa2. Here is how the company's portfolio is allocated:

And here are the largest 10 tenants of the company:

Operating Environment

Same here, the rate is Caa2:

Liquidity and access to capital

The company's operating expenses, excluding depreciation and amortization, are about 64.5% of revenue. EBITDA barely covers interest and dividends on preferred shares 1 time. Based on these indicators, all other things being equal, HPP deserves a credit rating of B2.

After calculating a score for market positioning, operating environment, and liquidity and adjusting all ratios based on the market-adjusted characteristics, we get a credit score of 15.28, or a solid B2 credit score.

Conclusion

HPP is a company with large total assets but in a difficult financial situation. It has high debts and is struggling to cover its expenses. Major credit agencies assess it as a risky investment. Although it offers high returns, investors should be careful because of the high risk.

data.title: Dollar General: Very Good Signs Of Turnaround, But It's Not Enough (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Summary

I gave a sell rating to Dollar General Corporation (NYSE:DG) in April, with my key thesis being that the demand backdrop has worsened, and I expect DG to face pressure at both the top and bottom lines. But after reviewing the recent performance, I believe I was too bearish. DG showed real improvement across the board, and the strength in discretionary categories is especially surprising. This, combined with a favorable shift in customer mix, warrants a rating upgrade.

The Turnaround Delivers a Strong Rebuttal

As mentioned, my sell rating on DG was based on the view that macro pressures and tariff risks would weigh on both sales and margins. But I may have been too pessimistic about it. DG’s recent 1Q25 results have proven me wrong on both fronts. For one, net sales grew 5.3% y/y to $10.4 billion, with same-store sales up 2.4%. More importantly, and this is what really shakes my sell rating conviction, is the discretionary performance. For the first time since 4Q21, all four major categories saw positive comparable sales growth (CSG), with home and seasonal merchandise CSG at or above 3% y/y. This is a direct “hit” to my previous concern that discretionary demand was structurally broken. The reality seems to be that DG’s updated merchandising playbook is working very well, and should that trend continue, DG is going to outperform my previous expectations.

Internally, DG has also shown that they have tightened up their ship. We can see this from the fact that gross margin expanded 78 bps to 31%, driven not by pricing but by operational improvements. Of which, the biggest driver was a 61 bps benefit from lower shrink. This 61 bps may not seem like a lot, but because it has 100% flow-through to the bottom line, it represents a sizeable mix of net margin (0.61% / 3.8% = ~16% mix). I have clearly underestimated just how quickly DG could turn the shrink problem around, and this reversal builds a lot of credibility in management’s turnaround ability.

Winning New Customers

Another development that convinced me that a sell rating is no longer appropriate is the shift in customer base. In the latest quarter, DG saw the highest mix of “trade-in” shoppers (basically middle- and higher-income consumers trading down) in the past four years. This is important because it directly addresses the negative narrative that DG has a sales mix heavily weighted towards lower-margin consumables. These new, wealthier shoppers are more likely to purchase higher-margin discretionary items, which is supported by management's comment that this cohort is allocating more of their spending to categories like home and seasonal goods. This not only helps improve overall gross margin but also diversifies DG's customer base, making top-line growth less reliant on its core, low-income demographic, which remains heavily under pressure.

Even more impressive is that these new customers are not just coming into the store to browse; they are visiting more often and spending more than last year’s cohort. This tells me DG’s value proposition is clearly resonating with the current value-conscious mindset, and that is a major win as it allows DG to gain market share in both dollars and units.

Core Consumer and Long-Term Concerns Remain

While the increasing mix of trade-in shoppers is encouraging, which, coupled with the headline performance, justifies me upgrading my rating from sell to hold, I don’t think it's enough to justify a buy rating. I still think it is important to note that demand from DG’s core low-income customer base continues to be poor. Per management, ~60% of these shoppers say they’re cutting back on necessities, and 25% report having less income vs. last year (refer to the 1Q25 earnings call for the source). As much as the trade-down motion is good, I see this dynamic as a strong limiting factor of how well sales can grow unless the macro backdrop improves.

Additionally, while 1Q25 was a step in the right direction, I am still wearing my cautious hat, in that this one strong quarter is not enough to form a trend. Also, I remind readers that this was against an easily comparable base. It is a good start, but there is still a long way to go for DG to restore its reputation, especially with the recent history of missing earnings expectations. It is also important to point out that the cost to build new stores has jumped >40% since 2019, which means there is a higher bar for new units to perform, and that adds an additional layer of uncertainty for DG to achieve its 6–7% long-term operating margin target by FY28.

Valuation

Valuation is another reason that prevented me from double-upgrading the stock to buy. When I wrote about DG in April, the stock traded at ~15x forward PE, but it is now at 18.5x. I think it's fair that the market has pushed up the multiple since DG really showed encouraging signs of turnaround, and that it is able to grow. However, at 18.5x, DG is trading slightly above its 10-year average of ~18x. For valuation to go higher from here, I think DG needs to show that this one quarter of turnaround is not a fluke and that it can sustain market share gains, grow top line, and maintain margins. Looking at how DG has performed in 1Q25, I’d say this is no longer implausible, but I have to wait for actual results to prove that this is true before I can turn bullish. For now, I think the stock stays range-bound.

Conclusion

I now give a hold rating for DG. DG has clearly shown early success in the turnaround, but I need more than one quarter to turn bullish. The core low-income customer remains under pressure, store build costs are still a headwind, and the stock now trades slightly above its historical average. I believe execution needs to stay strong for multiple quarters before the market will re-rate multiples higher.

data.title: Less Alarming U.K. Jobs Data Eases Pressure On The Bank Of England
data.description:
By James Smith

Last month’s UK jobs data revealed that May saw the largest fall in payrolled employee numbers on record (well, since 2014, and not including the peak of the pandemic). This month’s data shows that this didn’t actually happen.

May’s 109k drop was revised up to a more modest 25k decline, which is more in keeping with the trend we’ve seen over the past six months or so. June saw a slightly sharper 41k fall, but presumably that will be revised up later too.

None of that is hugely surprising; we saw something similar with the March data too. And a sharp decline in worker numbers would be totally inconsistent with the official redundancy numbers we get each week from the government, which have shown no discernible increase over the past few months.

That said, these payroll numbers - which are one of the few reliable ways of looking at the jobs market right now - have been falling for seven out of the past eight months. Employment is down by almost a percentage point since October on this metric, with more than half of the net job losses coming from hospitality or wholesale/retail. These are labour-intensive, lower-paid sectors which were more vulnerable to April’s National Insurance increase.

The fact that these sectors are dominated by small businesses may explain why it’s not showing up in the redundancy data, given that firms aren’t required to file a notice to the government if they have fewer than 20 staff on site.

The bottom line is that the jobs market is undoubtedly cooling - and judging by comparable vacancy data from hiring agency Indeed, it is cooler than in other major economies. But equally, the latest data shows things aren’t snowballing, which is what we tend to see during recessions.

That suggests pressure on wage growth should continue to ease this year. Annual private sector pay growth has slowed to 4.9% from 6% at the turn of the year. The three-month annualised rate, a better gauge of recent momentum, is at 3.7% - a much more comfortable level for the Bank. That’s consistent with what the BoE’s own “Decision Maker Panel” survey has shown over recent months too.

For now though, the combination of less worrisome jobs data and hotter inflation figures yesterday suggests the bar for the Bank of England accelerating cuts is still high. We expect cuts in August and November, and two further cuts next year.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: China Gold Market Update: Strong Investment In H1
data.description:
Gold consolidation continues but H1 strength intact

Gold prices saw limited moves in June. The LBMA Gold Price PM in USD climbed 0.3% while the SHAUPM in RMB fell mildly by 0.7% – due mainly to a stronger local currency against the dollar.

Nonetheless, the LBMA Gold Price in USD and the SHAUPM in RMB concluded H1 with their strongest performances since 2016, surging 23% and 21%, respectively (Chart 1). Based on our Gold Return Attribution Model, geopolitical risks and a weaker dollar were notable contributors to the gold price strength. Meanwhile, we believe continued central bank purchases further aided gold.

Chart 1: Gold saw its best H1 performance in both RMB and USD since 2016

H1 returns of the SHAUPM in RMB and the LBMA Gold Price PM in USD*

Wholesale demand stayed weak

China’s wholesale gold demand slowed further in June. Jewellery manufacturers, commercial banks and other market participants withdrew 90t during the month, 10% lower m/m. And while there was a mild 4% rise compared to a weak June in 2024, the month’s wholesale gold demand stayed well below its 10-year average (Chart 2).

Seasonal weakness, still tepid consumer confidence, and the elevated gold price continued to pressure gold jewellery consumption, leading to a cautious stance from retailers on restocking. Added to this was June’s cooling momentum in bar and coin investment as investors sat on the sidelines amid the range-bound gold price movement.

Chart 2: Wholesale gold demand weakened further in June*

Chart 3: SGE gold withdrawals in H1 stayed below the long-term average*

Chinese gold ETF flows turned positive in June, attracting RMB1bn (US$137mn) (Chart 4). With the US-China trade tensions easing and the RMB strengthening, safe-haven demand for gold cooled, resulting in limited changes in ETF flows.

During H1, Chinese gold ETFs registered their strongest semi-annual performance on record, adding RMB64bn (US$8.8bn). Inflows were driven by similar factors supported by bar and coin sales noted above. Chinese gold ETFs’ total AUM surged 116% during the first half, reaching RMB153bn (US$21bn) by the end of June. Meanwhile, collective holdings jumped 74% to 200t.

Chart 4: Chinese gold ETF demand surged in H1 to the highest on record

Collective holdings and monthly demand for Chinese gold ETFs*

Gold futures trading activity at the SHFE cooled in June (Chart 5), falling 39% m/m to 380t per day as the tepid price performance and narrowing volatility reduced trader interest.

Nonetheless, the SHFE gold futures trading volumes averaged 534t/day during H1, the highest semi-annual value on record, driven by factors already mentioned.

Chart 5: Average gold futures trading volumes in H1 reached the highest ever

Average semi-annual trading volumes of SHFE gold futures*

Non-stop PBoC gold purchase in H1

The PBoC reported a 2t gold purchase in June, the eighth consecutive monthly increase. China’s official gold holdings now stand at 2,299t (Chart 6).

China has announced non-stop gold purchases – of varying amounts – during the first half of 2025, totalling 19t. During this period, gold’s share of China’s total foreign exchange reserves rose from 5.5% in December 2024 to 6.7% at the end of June.

Chart 6: China’s official gold holdings have now risen for eight months in a row

Reported official gold holdings and gold as a percentage of total foreign exchange reserves*

Imports lowered in May

China imported 89t of gold in May on a net basis, according to the latest data from China Customs, down 21% m/m and 31% y/y. Basically, this reflects wholesale demand trends in the month: gold withdrawals from the SGE in May saw a sizable m/m fall. In general, weakening gold jewellery demand so far this year has weighed heavily on imports.

Chart 7: Imports fell in May

7108 gold imports under various regimes*
